abstract: Text classification serves as a cornerstone for numerous natural language processing applications. While traditional classifiers often depend on labor-intensive, handcrafted features—such as specialized dictionaries, external knowledge bases, and complex tree kernels—this study proposes a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) that learns directly from raw data. Our architecture utilizes a recurrent structure to capture broad contextual information during word representation learning, effectively reducing the noise typically introduced by standard window-based neural networks. Furthermore, we integrate a max-pooling layer to automatically identify and prioritize the most influential words within a text. Evaluating our model across four benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that this approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods, showing particularly significant improvements in document-level classification tasks.
